text.fullText:
I am writing to express strong opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency’s proposed 2026 revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 water quality certification regulations. The proposed rule would significantly weaken the ability of states and federally recognized Tribes to protect their waters—and therefore the health, safety, and welfare of their residents—by severely narrowing the scope of Section 401 reviews.

Under the current framework, states and Tribes have clear authority to evaluate whether an entire activity, not just a single discharge, will comply with applicable water quality requirements. This broader authority has been recognized for years as essential to ensuring that federally permitted projects do not harm local ecosystems or public health. Existing regulations allow certifying authorities to review whether an “activity” complies with “applicable water quality requirements,” giving them the necessary tools to prevent harmful environmental and community impacts.

However, EPA’s newly proposed 2026 rule would dramatically narrow this review authority and limit state and Tribal certifications only to discharges “from a point source into waters of the United States,” eliminating consideration of broader project impacts. Such a narrowing directly undermines the intent of Section 401, which Congress designed as a direct grant of power to states and Tribes to safeguard water quality within their borders.

By restricting Section 401 reviews to a narrow set of discharge‑related factors, the proposed rule prevents states and Tribes from addressing critical non‑discharging aspects of major infrastructure projects—such as pipeline routes, hydropower operations, habitat alteration, and cumulative impacts—that can materially harm water resources and public health. Analysts have noted that the proposal would limit the discretion of state agencies and narrow states’ ability to regulate non-discharging aspects of projects, ultimately benefiting developers at the expense of community health protections.

This rollback poses serious risks, especially for vulnerable communities that rely on state and Tribal agencies to advocate for their health, water security, and environmental well‑being. Weakening Section 401 will make it harder for local governments to stand against projects that may be ethically questionable, environmentally destructive, or dangerous for public health. States and Tribes must retain the authority to deny or condition certifications when projects threaten drinking water sources, fisheries, culturally significant resources, and the long‑term sustainability of watersheds.

For these reasons, I strongly urge EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and instead preserve the broader, activity‑wide review authority restored in the 2023 rulemaking. States and Tribes are indispensable co‑regulators in protecting our nation’s waters, and their ability to defend the health and interests of their citizens must not be diminished.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.
